parentQuestion: Which type of wave predominates during stage 1 NREM sleep?

scaffolds.q: Brain electrical activity can be measured with EEG (electroencephalography).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! EEG records voltage fluctuations from scalp electrodes - 'brain waves.'

scaffolds.q: Different brain wave frequencies correspond to different states of consciousness.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Fast waves = alert. Slow waves = relaxed or asleep. Frequency reflects brain state.

scaffolds.q: Beta waves (13-30 Hz) = alert, focused, concentrating. Fastest waves.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Beta is the signature of an active, engaged brain. Wide awake and thinking hard.

scaffolds.q: Alpha waves (8-13 Hz) = relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed. Slower than beta.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Alpha emerges when you close your eyes and relax. Idling frequency.

scaffolds.q: Theta waves (4-7 Hz) = light sleep, drowsy. Even slower.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Theta marks the transition from wakefulness to sleep - stage 1 NREM.

scaffolds.q: Delta waves (0.5-4 Hz) = deep sleep (stage 3 NREM). Slowest waves.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Delta = slow-wave sleep. High amplitude, highly synchronized, deep restorative sleep.

scaffolds.q: Stage 1 NREM is the LIGHTEST sleep stage - drifting off, easily awakened.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! N1 is the transition zone between awake and asleep. Just drifting off.

scaffolds.q: Stage 1 NREM (N1) is dominated by theta waves (4-7 Hz).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Slower than alpha (relaxed awake) but faster than delta (deep sleep).

scaffolds.q: The answer is THETA - the wave that predominates in stage 1 NREM.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not alpha (relaxed wakefulness), not beta (alert), not delta (deep sleep). Theta waves dominate stage 1 NREM - the lightest sleep stage.
